---
lang: en-US
title: Inference

format:
  html:
    number-sections: true
---

# Load the Trained Model

This section covers how to load a trained segmentation model from the MLflow
Model Registry and apply it to a Sentinel-2 image to produce land-cover predictions.

## From MLflow

MLflow lets you version and compare training runs, then promote the best model
to a **Model Registry**. Follow these steps to register your model:

1. In the top left, select **Model Training**.
2. Go to **Experiments** and open `"funathon-2026-project3"`.
3. In the **Runs** section, compare runs using the logged metrics and select
   the best-performing one.
4. Click **Register model**, name it `"segmentation-sentinel2-model"`, and confirm.
5. Verify it appears in the **Model Registry**.

Once registered, load the model using the `get_model()` utility:
```{python}
#| eval: false

import os
from utils import get_model, get_normalization_metrics

os.environ["MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI"] = "https://projet-formation-mlflow.user.lab.sspcloud.fr"
os.environ["MLFLOW_TRACKING_USERNAME"] = "projet-formation"
os.environ["MLFLOW_TRACKING_PASSWORD"] = "xxx"

model_name = "segmentation-sentinel2-model"
model_version = "2"
mlflow_tracking_uri = os.getenv("MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI")

model = get_model(model_name, model_version, mlflow_tracking_uri)
```

`get_model()` wraps `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model` and raises a descriptive
exception if the model or version cannot be found.

## Retrieve model metadata

The training run stores key hyperparameters as MLflow params. Retrieve them to
configure the inference pipeline consistently with how the model was trained:
```{python}
#| eval: false

import mlflow

run = mlflow.get_run(model.metadata.run_id)

n_bands = int(run.data.params["n_bands"])
tiles_size = int(run.data.params["tiles_size"])
augment_size = int(run.data.params["augment_size"])
module_name = run.data.params["module_name"]

normalization_mean, normalization_std = get_normalization_metrics(model, n_bands)

print(f"n_bands={n_bands}, tiles_size={tiles_size}, augment_size={augment_size}")
print(f"mean={normalization_mean}")
print(f"std={normalization_std}")
```

- **`tiles_size`**: the spatial resolution (in pixels) the model was trained on.
- **`augment_size`**: if different from `tiles_size`, a resize is automatically
  inserted in the preprocessing pipeline.
- **`normalization_mean` / `normalization_std`**: per-band statistics used to
  standardise the image before inference.

# Apply the Model

## Predict on a single Sentinel-2 image

The `predict()` utility handles the full pipeline — loading the image from S3,
preprocessing, tiling if needed, and assembling the result back into a
`SegmentationLabeledSatelliteImage`:
```{python}
#| eval: false

from utils import predict, get_file_system

fs = get_file_system()

image_path = (
    "projet-formation/diffusion/funathon/2026/project3/"
    "data/images/LU000/2024/3034500_2011690.tif"
)

lsi = predict(
    images=image_path,
    model=model,
    tiles_size=tiles_size,
    augment_size=augment_size,
    n_bands=n_bands,
    normalization_mean=normalization_mean,
    normalization_std=normalization_std,
    module_name=module_name,
)
```

Internally, `predict()`:

1. Opens the image from S3 via `/vsis3/` (no local download needed).
2. Normalises and resizes with `albumentations`.
3. Splits the image into tiles of `tiles_size × tiles_size` if the image is
   larger, then reassembles the predictions with `make_mosaic`.
4. Returns a `SegmentationLabeledSatelliteImage` containing the raw logits.

## Produce the class mask

Convert the raw logits to a 2D array of predicted class IDs with `produce_mask()`:
```{python}
#| eval: false

from utils import produce_mask

lsi.label = produce_mask(lsi.label, module_name)

print(f"Mask shape : {lsi.label.shape}")
print(f"Classes found : {set(lsi.label.flatten().tolist())}")
```

`produce_mask()` applies `torch.argmax` along the class dimension, returning
an integer array of shape `(H, W)` where each value is the predicted class ID
(1–10 for CLC+ Backbone).

## Display the prediction
```{python}
#| eval: false

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap
from matplotlib.patches import Patch

classes = [
    ("Sealed (1)",                              "#FF0100"),
    ("Woody – needle leaved trees (2)",         "#238B23"),
    ("Woody – broadleaved deciduous (3)",       "#80FF00"),
    ("Woody – broadleaved evergreen (4)",       "#00FF00"),
    ("Low-growing woody plants (5)",            "#804000"),
    ("Permanent herbaceous (6)",                "#CCF24E"),
    ("Periodically herbaceous (7)",             "#FEFF80"),
    ("Lichens and mosses (8)",                  "#FF81FF"),
    ("Non- and sparsely-vegetated (9)",         "#BFBFBF"),
    ("Water (10)",                              "#0080FF"),
]

cmap = ListedColormap([color for _, color in classes])

# RGB composite for visualisation (bands 4, 3, 2)
rgb = np.transpose(lsi.satellite_image.array[[3, 2, 1]], (1, 2, 0)).astype(np.float32)
p98 = np.percentile(rgb, 98)
rgb = np.clip(rgb / p98, 0, 1)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 6))

axes[0].imshow(rgb)
axes[0].set_title("Sentinel-2 RGB (B4, B3, B2)")
axes[0].axis("off")

axes[1].imshow(lsi.label, cmap=cmap, vmin=1, vmax=10)
axes[1].set_title("Predicted land cover")
axes[1].axis("off")

legend_elements = [
    Patch(facecolor=color, edgecolor="black", label=label)
    for label, color in classes
]

fig.legend(
    handles=legend_elements,
    loc="center right",
    bbox_to_anchor=(1.35, 0.5),
    frameon=True,
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Convert predictions to polygons

`create_geojson_from_mask()` vectorises the class mask into a GeoDataFrame of
polygons, each carrying its `label` (class ID). It writes the mask to a
temporary GeoTIFF to preserve the georeference, then calls
`rasterio.features.shapes` to extract contiguous regions:
```{python}
#| eval: false

from utils import create_geojson_from_mask

gdf_pred = create_geojson_from_mask(lsi)

print(f"{len(gdf_pred)} polygons extracted")
print(gdf_pred.head())

gdf_nuts.to_parquet("predictions_LU000_3034500_2011690_2024.parquet")
```

::: {.callout-note}
Pixels with class value `0` (no-data / background) are automatically excluded
from the output GeoDataFrame.
:::

Plot the polygons coloured by class on top of the satellite image:
```{python}
#| eval: false

import geopandas as gpd

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))
ax.imshow(rgb)

gdf_pred.plot(
    column="label",
    cmap=cmap,
    vmin=1,
    vmax=10,
    alpha=0.5,
    ax=ax,
    legend=False,
)

ax.set_title("Predicted polygons overlay")
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# API

A REST API has been deployed to run inference at scale for a given NUTS3/year
pair. It wraps the same `predict()` / `create_geojson_from_mask()` pipeline
with an S3 cache layer, and exposes three endpoints:

| Endpoint | Description |
|---|---|
| `GET /find_image` | Returns the image filename that contains a GPS point |
| `GET /predict_image` | Predicts a single image by path; optionally returns polygons |
| `GET /predict_nuts` | Predicts all images for a NUTS3/year pair |

Call the NUTS-level endpoint and load the result as a GeoDataFrame:
```{python}
#| eval: false

import requests
import geopandas as gpd

api_url = "https://projet-formation-api.user.lab.sspcloud.fr"

response = requests.get(
    f"{api_url}/predict_nuts",
    params={"nuts_id": "LU000", "year": 2024},
)
response.raise_for_status()

gdf_nuts = gpd.GeoDataFrame.from_features(
    response.json()["predictions"], crs="EPSG:3035"
)

print(f"{len(gdf_nuts)} polygons received")
gdf_nuts.head()

gdf_nuts.to_parquet("predictions_LU000_2024.parquet")
```

The response is a **GeoJSON FeatureCollection** serialised as a JSON string
inside the `"predictions"` key.
